DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-30-2019 Overruled
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Kelly Matayoshi, Esq. 415-954-4400 of Farella Braun & Martel LLP for Defendant Visa Inc. through Court Call.
Stephanie Awanyai, Esq. 424-204-4400 of Ballard Spahr LLP for Defendant Citibank N.A & Citigroup through Court Call.
Jmaes Greenblatt, Esq. 619-578-4975 of Morris Sullican & Lemkul LLP for Defendant Infusion Lounge Licensing Corp. through Court Call.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT BIJOU-CENTURY, LLC'S DEMURRER TO PLAINTIFF'S 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Defendant Bijou-Century, LLC's demurrer to the first cause of action for sexual battery in violation of Civil Code, 1708.5 is overruled. The amended complaint alleges sufficient ultimate facts showing that plaintiff was a victim of sexual battery and defendant is vicariously liable. (Amended Complaint, 16, 21, 23, 37, 38, 40, 41, 42, 44, 49, 63, 65, 68, 90, 94, 95, 96, 97, 99, 108, 109, 11, 114.) A factual issue remains whether plaintiff was sexually battered. Whether plaintiff lacks memory of the specifics of the incident is not dispositive, since it is conceivable that other evidence could support the claim. The allegation that plaintiff's roommate found plaintiff stripped naked and in the presence of half-naked strangers supports the claim. (Amended Complaint, 65.) Defendant also raises a factual issue regarding vicarious liability. The amended complaint is littered with allegations that the co-defendants were acting at defendant's behest, whether as employees, agents, or otherwise. The Court further notes that defendant allegedly benefited financially from the transactions. (Amended Complaint, 41, 99.) Lastly, defendant's factual argument that plaintiff cannot establish vicarious liability or that the co-defendants were not acting within the course and scope of their employment is based on cases decided on summary judgment, not demurrer. (See Lisa M. v Henry Mayo Newhall Memorial Hospital (1995) 12 Cal.4th 291; Farmers Insurance Group v. County of Santa Clara (1995) 11 Cal.4th 992; Z.V. v. County of Riverside (2015) 238 Cal.App.4th 889; Montague v. AMN Healthcare, Inc. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th1515; Yamaguchi v. Harnsmut (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 472.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Motion To Strike First Amended Complaint And References To John Doe Therein; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof
Off Calendar Oct-30-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Kelly Matayoshi, Esq. 415-954-4400 of Farella Braun & Martel LLP for Defendant Visa Inc. through Court Call.
Stephanie Awanyai, Esq. 424-204-4400 of Ballard Spahr LLP for Defendant Citibank N.A & Citigroup through Court Call.
Jmaes Greenblatt, Esq. 619-578-4975 of Morris Sullican & Lemkul LLP for Defendant Infusion Lounge Licensing Corp. through Court Call.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT BIJOU-CENTURY, LLC'S Motion To Strike First Amended Complaint And References To John Doe Therein.

Defendant Bijou-Century, LLC's motion to strike is denied and plaintiff is granted leave of court to proceed anonymously.

"The judicial use of 'Doe plaintiffs' to protect legitimate privacy rights has gained wide currency, particularly given the rapidity and ubiquity of disclosures over the World Wide Web." (Starbucks Corp. v. Superior Court (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1436, 1452 fn. 7); see, e.g., Doe v. City of Los Angeles (2007) 42 Cal.4th 531 [former Boy Scouts sued under pseudonym based on allegations that city police officer sexually assaulted them while they were teenagers]; Doe v. Superior Court (Luster) (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 750, 754 [rape victim permitted to file complaint under fictitious name in order to preserve her anonymity].) Federal courts allow parties to use pseudonyms "in special circumstances when the party's need for anonymity outweighs prejudice to the opposing party and the public's interest in knowing the party's identity." (Does I thru XXIII v. Advanced Textile Corp. (9th Cir. 2000) 214 F.3d 1058, 1068.) "Applying this balancing test, courts have permitted plaintiffs to use pseudonyms in three situations: (1) when identification creates a risk of retaliatory physical or mental harm, [citations]; (2) when anonymity is necessary 'to preserve privacy in a matter of sensitive and highly personal nature,' [citations] and (3) when the anonymous party is 'compelled to admit [his or her] intention to engage in illegal conduct, thereby risking criminal prosecution.'" (Id.; see also Doe v. Lincoln Unified School Dist. (2010) 188 Cal.App.4th 758, 767.)

This case presents a clear example of the need to conceal a party's identity to preserve his privacy in a matter of a highly personal nature. Plaintiff alleges that he was involuntarily drugged, kidnapped, stripped naked, and taken advantage of financially. (Amended Complaint, 37, 40, 43, 65-68.) Such allegations open plaintiff up to public ridicule and humiliation and the public has little to no interest in knowing plaintiff's identity. (Doe Decl. 9.) Defendant's claim of prejudice is spurious as plaintiff has already disclosed his identity for purposes of investigation, and has also offered to cooperate in discovery so as to minimize any impact of his proceeding anonymously. (Acikalin Decl., 6-7, 9, 10.) The only legitimate argument that defendant makes in its motion to strike is that plaintiff should have obtained a court order before proceeding anonymously. As Judge Ulmer previously noted, such an order is necessary. (See 10/9/19 order denying motion to compel arbitration without prejudice). The Court now grants plaintiff permission to proceed anonymously.

Even though the Court concludes that defendant has no legitimate basis for its motion to strike, the Court denies plaintiff's request for sanctions. As noted, plaintiff should have obtained an order allowing anonymity concurrently when he filed his complaint.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT CITIGROUP INC., CITIBANK, N.A.'s Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action, as follows:

Defendant Citibank, N.A.'s motion to compel arbitration is denied without prejudice. The motion raises an unresolved threshold issue: whether plaintiff may sue using a fictitious name. This is permitted in "extraordinary circumstances," but a "prior court order is required." (Edmon & Karnow, California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (TRG 2016) 6.90.10; see also Starbucks Corp. v. Sup. Ct. (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1436, 1452 n.7 ("judicial use of Doe plaintiffs"; "order which maintains the confidentiality of John Doe's identity" (emphasis added; quote marks deleted).) The register of actions indicates that this issue has been raised in the case, but not resolved by court order.

Order signed in open court.
